title: Leg Ulcer — Venous vs Arterial
oneLiner: Chronic wounds on lower legs; venous (oedema, shallow, medial) vs arterial (painful, punched-out, lateral/toes); wrong compression in arterial can cause limb loss.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute limb ischaemia** – sudden pain, pallor, pulselessness, paralysis, paraesthesia, poikilothermia. **Single finding:** no arterial Doppler signal (handheld Doppler) + severe pain. Urgent vascular referral; no compression.
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – rapid spreading redness, crepitus (gas on palpation), severe pain out of proportion to exam, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension). **Single finding:** skin blistering/crepitus + lactate >2 mmol/L. Immediate surgical debridement, broad-spectrum antibiotics.
- **Sepsis from infected ulcer** – spreading erythema >2 cm from wound edge, fever, raised lactate, hypotension. **Single finding:** NEWS2 ≥5 plus purulent discharge. Start IV antibiotics and source control.
sections.Presentation:
- **Venous ulcer:**
  - Location: medial malleolus (typically), sometimes lateral or anterior shin
  - Edge: shallow, irregular, sloping, often granulation tissue
  - Base: moist, yellow slough or healthy granulation
  - Surrounding skin: haemosiderin staining (brown-bronze), lipodermatosclerosis (woody induration), eczema, varicose veins
  - Pain: minimal or mild aching, relieved by elevation; no rest pain
  - Oedema: heavy, pitting, worse at day-end
  - Pulses: palpable dorsalis pedis/posterior tibial (unless mixed) — **never absent in pure venous**
- **Arterial ulcer:**
  - Location: lateral malleolus, toes, heel, pressure points (e.g. metatarsal heads)
  - Edge: punched-out, sharply demarcated
  - Base: pale, necrotic black eschar, or deep tendon exposure
  - Surrounding skin: shiny, hairless, cool, thin; may have dependent rubor (red when dangling)
  - Pain: severe, **worse at night**, relieved by hanging leg over bed; rest pain common
  - Oedema: minimal unless mixed; foot often cold
  - Pulses: **absent or weak** — dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial, popliteal, femoral
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Venous ulcer** (most common ~70%) — oedema, haemosiderin, palpable pulses
- **Arterial ulcer** — rest pain, absent pulses, cool foot
- **Mixed venous-arterial** — features of both; **always check ABPI** — if 0.5-0.8, proceed cautiously with reduced compression
- **Diabetic foot ulcer** — neuropathic (insensate, callus, plantar) or ischaemic (pulseless); check monofilament and pedal pulses
- **Vasculitic ulcer** (e.g. livedoid vasculopathy, rheumatoid) — punched-out, painful, purpuric border; check ESR/CRP, ANA, ANCA
- **Pyoderma gangrenosum** — rapidly expanding painful ulcer with undermined violaceous edge; associated with IBD; avoid debridement (pathergy)
- **Malignancy** (squamous cell carcinoma in chronic ulcer – Marjolin's ulcer) — rolled, raised edge, induration, bleeding; biopsy if non-healing >6 months
- **Pressure ulcer** — over bony prominences (heel, sacrum); associated with immobility
- **Splitter:** **ABPI measurement** — if ABPI >0.9 and pulses palpable → mainly venous; if ABPI <0.5 or absent pulses → arterial. Also: pain character (rest pain suggests arterial).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Handheld Doppler: ABPI = highest ankle systolic pressure / brachial systolic pressure. Normal >1.0; claudication 0.5-0.9; critical ischaemia <0.5. **Do not apply compression if ABPI <0.8.**
  - Wound swab (MCS) if signs of infection (purulence, spreading erythema, malodour)
  - Pulse palpation (femoral, popliteal, DP/PT) — document
  - Sensory exam: monofilament for diabetic neuropathy
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (infection, anaemia), CRP (inflammation), U&E (renal function in elderly), HbA1c (undiagnosed diabetes)
  - If suspect vasculitis: ESR, ANA, ANCA, complement
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Duplex ultrasound** (venous + arterial) — identifies reflux, obstruction, stenosis
  - Second-line: CT angiogram or MR angiogram if revascularisation considered (arterial)
  - Plain X-ray: if suspected osteomyelitis (ask for views of foot/ankle)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - CEAP classification (Clinical, Etiology, Anatomy, Pathophysiology) for venous disease — C0-C6; C6 = active ulcer
  - Fontaine (arterial): Stage IV = rest pain/ulcer/gangrene
- **Never-miss test:** **ABPI** — omission leads to inappropriate compression causing limb ischaemia/necrosis. Also: **if diabetic, check foot sensations** to differentiate neuropathic component.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ward arrival):**
  - If **arterial ulcer** or acute ischaemia: **nil by mouth** for potential urgent angioplasty/bypass; keep leg dependent (not elevated); urgent vascular referral; do **not** apply compression
  - If **venous ulcer** with ABPI >0.8: leg elevation (30°, avoid pressure on heel), apply **multi-layer compression bandage** (e.g. short stretch) — only if ABPI >0.8. Clean with saline, simple non-adherent dressing
  - If **infected** (cellulitis, fever): send blood cultures, start IV antibiotics (see Drugs). **Do not wait for swab result if systemically unwell.**
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV/PO qds; if arterial rest pain, add morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (monitor sedation, respiratory rate)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Venous:** replace compression bandages every 5-7 days; if heavily exuding, use foam/alginate dressing. Continue elevation when in bed. Encourage ankle exercises. Refer to leg ulcer clinic/community nurse.
  - **Arterial:** keep leg down; wound care with hydrogel or iodine for sloughy/necrotic base; avoid defbridement of black eschar unless clearly infected (needs vascular assessment first). Start antiplatelet (aspirin 75mg OD or clopidogrel 75mg) and statin (atorvastatin 40mg or [check local policy]) if not contraindicated. Optimise BP (<140/90), stop smoking. Refer to vascular surgeon.
  - **Mixed:** use reduced compression (e.g. 1-layer or [check local policy]) if ABPI 0.5-0.8; refer to vascular
  - All: wound bed preparation (debride slough, manage exudate). Consider **pentoxifylline** 400mg PO TDS for venous ulcer healing (NICE recommendation) — [check local policy]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Venous: lifelong compression hosiery (class 1-3 based on ABPI, ankle circumference). Weight loss, leg exercises, varicose vein treatment if appropriate (endovenous ablation or surgery).
  - Arterial: revascularisation (angioplasty, bypass) if symptoms severe; then compression may be considered after revasc. Continue antiplatelet/statins.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: no specific required unless dehydrated; if fasting for procedure, IV 1L 0.9% saline over 8h
  - VTE prophylaxis: **only if ABPI >0.8 and no sign of critical ischaemia** — enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (risk of haematoma over arterial lesions; consider alternative if high bleeding risk)
  - Diet/NBM: if potential intervention (angiogram, surgery) — NBM from 6 hours; otherwise normal diet with high protein (wound healing)
  - Observations: baseline 4-hourly for first 24h (NEWS2); if infection/sepsis, more frequent
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Antimicrobials (infected ulcer):**
  - First-line: **Flucoxacillin** 500mg-1g IV q6h (mild PO 500mg q6h) for cellulitis without MRSA risk
  - If penicillin allergy or MRSA risk: **Clindamycin** 600mg IV q8h (or PO 300-450mg q6h)
  - If mixed flora (diabetic foot): **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2g IV TDS (or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe) — [check local policy]
  - Duration: 7-14 days based on response; step-down to oral when improving
- **Antiplatelets (arterial):**
  - **Aspirin** 75mg PO OD (indefinite)
  - **Clopidogrel** 75mg PO OD if aspirin intolerant
- **Statins (arterial):**
  - **Atorvastatin** 40-80mg PO OD (target LDL <2.0 mmol/L or 50% reduction)
- **Pentoxifylline** (venous ulcer):
  - 400mg PO TDS (with food) — beware GI upset, contraindicated in recent haemorrhage, major bleeding risk
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV qds
  - Morphine 2.5-5mg IV/SC PRN (arterial rest pain)
  - Gabapentin or amitriptyline for neuropathic pain if diabetic — start low, titrate
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Compression bandages in ABPI <0.8 → necrosis
  - NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) — use cautiously; can worsen renal function in elderly; avoid in vascular disease (platelet dysfunction)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g. 20mg SC OD)
  - Pentoxifylline: caution in severe renal/hepatic impairment — consider alternative
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Compression bandages applied without ABPI** — leads to acute limb ischaemia, amputation, death. Always document ABPI before dressing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cellulitis, abscess, osteomyelitis (in deep ulcer over bone)
  - Acute limb ischaemia (from compression or embolisation)
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Haemorrhage from eroded vessels (rare, emergency)
- **Chronic:**
  - Non-healing → amputation (arterial)
  - Malignant transformation (Marjolin's ulcer, squamous cell) — biopsy any non-healing ulcer >3 months
  - Chronic pain, depression, social isolation
  - Contact dermatitis (to dressings, antiseptics)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Venous:** ~70% heal with good compression at 6 months; recurrence >50% if compression stockings not worn. Good prognosis if adherent.
- **Arterial:** poor healing without revascularisation; 20-40% require major amputation within 1 year if critical ischaemia. Life expectancy reduced due to PAD (MI/stroke risk).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure ABPI before compression** — if unsure, get vascular opinion before bandaging.
- **If patient hangs leg over bed at night for pain relief** → suspect critical limb ischaemia; do not elevate; call vascular.
- **Document pedal pulses** clearly (present? doppler? ankle-brachial index?) — guides all future management.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never apply high compression to an undifferentiated leg ulcer** — arterial disease will worsen, leading to necrosis and amputation.
- **Do not assume all ulcers are venous** — a painful ulcer with absent pulses is arterial until proven otherwise.
- **Avoid debriding a black eschar in an arterial ulcer without revascularisation** — it will enlarge and worsen.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening pain, especially dangling leg no longer helps; spreading redness beyond wound edge; new numbness, coldness, or colour change in foot; fever; new swelling or redness up the leg.
- **Follow-up:** Community leg ulcer clinic (weekly for venous); vascular clinic within 2 weeks (or sooner if rest pain) for arterial; district nurse for wound care.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / vascular with:** Sudden severe pain + pulseless foot (acute ischaemia) — immediate; NEWS2 ≥5; lactate >2.0 mmol/L; new crepitus or blistering (necrotising fasciitis) — do not wait; call surgical team or crash team if hypotensive.
- **Call vascular surgeon on-call for:** ABPI <0.5 with rest pain/ulcer; non-palpable pulses; increasing pain despite analgesia.

rawMarkdown:
# Leg Ulcer — Venous vs Arterial
**One-liner:** Chronic wounds on lower legs; venous (oedema, shallow, medial) vs arterial (painful, punched-out, lateral/toes); wrong compression in arterial can cause limb loss.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute limb ischaemia** – sudden pain, pallor, pulselessness, paralysis, paraesthesia, poikilothermia. **Single finding:** no arterial Doppler signal (handheld Doppler) + severe pain. Urgent vascular referral; no compression.
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – rapid spreading redness, crepitus (gas on palpation), severe pain out of proportion to exam, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension). **Single finding:** skin blistering/crepitus + lactate >2 mmol/L. Immediate surgical debridement, broad-spectrum antibiotics.
- **Sepsis from infected ulcer** – spreading erythema >2 cm from wound edge, fever, raised lactate, hypotension. **Single finding:** NEWS2 ≥5 plus purulent discharge. Start IV antibiotics and source control.

### Presentation
- **Venous ulcer:**
  - Location: medial malleolus (typically), sometimes lateral or anterior shin
  - Edge: shallow, irregular, sloping, often granulation tissue
  - Base: moist, yellow slough or healthy granulation
  - Surrounding skin: haemosiderin staining (brown-bronze), lipodermatosclerosis (woody induration), eczema, varicose veins
  - Pain: minimal or mild aching, relieved by elevation; no rest pain
  - Oedema: heavy, pitting, worse at day-end
  - Pulses: palpable dorsalis pedis/posterior tibial (unless mixed) — **never absent in pure venous**
- **Arterial ulcer:**
  - Location: lateral malleolus, toes, heel, pressure points (e.g. metatarsal heads)
  - Edge: punched-out, sharply demarcated
  - Base: pale, necrotic black eschar, or deep tendon exposure
  - Surrounding skin: shiny, hairless, cool, thin; may have dependent rubor (red when dangling)
  - Pain: severe, **worse at night**, relieved by hanging leg over bed; rest pain common
  - Oedema: minimal unless mixed; foot often cold
  - Pulses: **absent or weak** — dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial, popliteal, femoral

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Venous ulcer** (most common ~70%) — oedema, haemosiderin, palpable pulses
- **Arterial ulcer** — rest pain, absent pulses, cool foot
- **Mixed venous-arterial** — features of both; **always check ABPI** — if 0.5-0.8, proceed cautiously with reduced compression
- **Diabetic foot ulcer** — neuropathic (insensate, callus, plantar) or ischaemic (pulseless); check monofilament and pedal pulses
- **Vasculitic ulcer** (e.g. livedoid vasculopathy, rheumatoid) — punched-out, painful, purpuric border; check ESR/CRP, ANA, ANCA
- **Pyoderma gangrenosum** — rapidly expanding painful ulcer with undermined violaceous edge; associated with IBD; avoid debridement (pathergy)
- **Malignancy** (squamous cell carcinoma in chronic ulcer – Marjolin's ulcer) — rolled, raised edge, induration, bleeding; biopsy if non-healing >6 months
- **Pressure ulcer** — over bony prominences (heel, sacrum); associated with immobility
- **Splitter:** **ABPI measurement** — if ABPI >0.9 and pulses palpable → mainly venous; if ABPI <0.5 or absent pulses → arterial. Also: pain character (rest pain suggests arterial).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Handheld Doppler: ABPI = highest ankle systolic pressure / brachial systolic pressure. Normal >1.0; claudication 0.5-0.9; critical ischaemia <0.5. **Do not apply compression if ABPI <0.8.**
  - Wound swab (MCS) if signs of infection (purulence, spreading erythema, malodour)
  - Pulse palpation (femoral, popliteal, DP/PT) — document
  - Sensory exam: monofilament for diabetic neuropathy
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (infection, anaemia), CRP (inflammation), U&E (renal function in elderly), HbA1c (undiagnosed diabetes)
  - If suspect vasculitis: ESR, ANA, ANCA, complement
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Duplex ultrasound** (venous + arterial) — identifies reflux, obstruction, stenosis
  - Second-line: CT angiogram or MR angiogram if revascularisation considered (arterial)
  - Plain X-ray: if suspected osteomyelitis (ask for views of foot/ankle)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - CEAP classification (Clinical, Etiology, Anatomy, Pathophysiology) for venous disease — C0-C6; C6 = active ulcer
  - Fontaine (arterial): Stage IV = rest pain/ulcer/gangrene
- **Never-miss test:** **ABPI** — omission leads to inappropriate compression causing limb ischaemia/necrosis. Also: **if diabetic, check foot sensations** to differentiate neuropathic component.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ward arrival):**
  - If **arterial ulcer** or acute ischaemia: **nil by mouth** for potential urgent angioplasty/bypass; keep leg dependent (not elevated); urgent vascular referral; do **not** apply compression
  - If **venous ulcer** with ABPI >0.8: leg elevation (30°, avoid pressure on heel), apply **multi-layer compression bandage** (e.g. short stretch) — only if ABPI >0.8. Clean with saline, simple non-adherent dressing
  - If **infected** (cellulitis, fever): send blood cultures, start IV antibiotics (see Drugs). **Do not wait for swab result if systemically unwell.**
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV/PO qds; if arterial rest pain, add morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (monitor sedation, respiratory rate)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Venous:** replace compression bandages every 5-7 days; if heavily exuding, use foam/alginate dressing. Continue elevation when in bed. Encourage ankle exercises. Refer to leg ulcer clinic/community nurse.
  - **Arterial:** keep leg down; wound care with hydrogel or iodine for sloughy/necrotic base; avoid defbridement of black eschar unless clearly infected (needs vascular assessment first). Start antiplatelet (aspirin 75mg OD or clopidogrel 75mg) and statin (atorvastatin 40mg or [check local policy]) if not contraindicated. Optimise BP (<140/90), stop smoking. Refer to vascular surgeon.
  - **Mixed:** use reduced compression (e.g. 1-layer or [check local policy]) if ABPI 0.5-0.8; refer to vascular
  - All: wound bed preparation (debride slough, manage exudate). Consider **pentoxifylline** 400mg PO TDS for venous ulcer healing (NICE recommendation) — [check local policy]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Venous: lifelong compression hosiery (class 1-3 based on ABPI, ankle circumference). Weight loss, leg exercises, varicose vein treatment if appropriate (endovenous ablation or surgery).
  - Arterial: revascularisation (angioplasty, bypass) if symptoms severe; then compression may be considered after revasc. Continue antiplatelet/statins.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: no specific required unless dehydrated; if fasting for procedure, IV 1L 0.9% saline over 8h
  - VTE prophylaxis: **only if ABPI >0.8 and no sign of critical ischaemia** — enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (risk of haematoma over arterial lesions; consider alternative if high bleeding risk)
  - Diet/NBM: if potential intervention (angiogram, surgery) — NBM from 6 hours; otherwise normal diet with high protein (wound healing)
  - Observations: baseline 4-hourly for first 24h (NEWS2); if infection/sepsis, more frequent

### Drugs & Doses
- **Antimicrobials (infected ulcer):**
  - First-line: **Flucoxacillin** 500mg-1g IV q6h (mild PO 500mg q6h) for cellulitis without MRSA risk
  - If penicillin allergy or MRSA risk: **Clindamycin** 600mg IV q8h (or PO 300-450mg q6h)
  - If mixed flora (diabetic foot): **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2g IV TDS (or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe) — [check local policy]
  - Duration: 7-14 days based on response; step-down to oral when improving
- **Antiplatelets (arterial):**
  - **Aspirin** 75mg PO OD (indefinite)
  - **Clopidogrel** 75mg PO OD if aspirin intolerant
- **Statins (arterial):**
  - **Atorvastatin** 40-80mg PO OD (target LDL <2.0 mmol/L or 50% reduction)
- **Pentoxifylline** (venous ulcer):
  - 400mg PO TDS (with food) — beware GI upset, contraindicated in recent haemorrhage, major bleeding risk
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g PO/IV qds
  - Morphine 2.5-5mg IV/SC PRN (arterial rest pain)
  - Gabapentin or amitriptyline for neuropathic pain if diabetic — start low, titrate
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Compression bandages in ABPI <0.8 → necrosis
  - NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) — use cautiously; can worsen renal function in elderly; avoid in vascular disease (platelet dysfunction)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 (e.g. 20mg SC OD)
  - Pentoxifylline: caution in severe renal/hepatic impairment — consider alternative
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Compression bandages applied without ABPI** — leads to acute limb ischaemia, amputation, death. Always document ABPI before dressing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cellulitis, abscess, osteomyelitis (in deep ulcer over bone)
  - Acute limb ischaemia (from compression or embolisation)
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Haemorrhage from eroded vessels (rare, emergency)
- **Chronic:**
  - Non-healing → amputation (arterial)
  - Malignant transformation (Marjolin's ulcer, squamous cell) — biopsy any non-healing ulcer >3 months
  - Chronic pain, depression, social isolation
  - Contact dermatitis (to dressings, antiseptics)

### Prognosis
- **Venous:** ~70% heal with good compression at 6 months; recurrence >50% if compression stockings not worn. Good prognosis if adherent.
- **Arterial:** poor healing without revascularisation; 20-40% require major amputation within 1 year if critical ischaemia. Life expectancy reduced due to PAD (MI/stroke risk).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure ABPI before compression** — if unsure, get vascular opinion before bandaging.
- **If patient hangs leg over bed at night for pain relief** → suspect critical limb ischaemia; do not elevate; call vascular.
- **Document pedal pulses** clearly (present? doppler? ankle-brachial index?) — guides all future management.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never apply high compression to an undifferentiated leg ulcer** — arterial disease will worsen, leading to necrosis and amputation.
- **Do not assume all ulcers are venous** — a painful ulcer with absent pulses is arterial until proven otherwise.
- **Avoid debriding a black eschar in an arterial ulcer without revascularisation** — it will enlarge and worsen.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening pain, especially dangling leg no longer helps; spreading redness beyond wound edge; new numbness, coldness, or colour change in foot; fever; new swelling or redness up the leg.
- **Follow-up:** Community leg ulcer clinic (weekly for venous); vascular clinic within 2 weeks (or sooner if rest pain) for arterial; district nurse for wound care.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / vascular with:** Sudden severe pain + pulseless foot (acute ischaemia) — immediate; NEWS2 ≥5; lactate >2.0 mmol/L; new crepitus or blistering (necrotising fasciitis) — do not wait; call surgical team or crash team if hypotensive.
- **Call vascular surgeon on-call for:** ABPI <0.5 with rest pain/ulcer; non-palpable pulses; increasing pain despite analgesia.
